text: Single-cell RNA-seq(scRNA-seq) data simulation is critical for evaluating computational methods for analysing scRNA-seq data especially when ground truth is experimentally unattainable. The reliability of evaluation depends on the ability of simulation methods to capture properties of experimental data. However, while many scRNA-seq data simulation methods have been proposed, a systematic evaluation of these methods is lacking. Here, we develop a comprehensive evaluation framework, SimBench, including a kernel density estimation measure to benchmark 12 simulation methods through 35 scRNA-seq experimental datasets. We evaluate the simulation methods on a panel of data properties, ability to maintain biological signals, scalability and applicability. Our benchmark uncovers performance differences among the methods and highlights the varying difficulties in simulating data characteristics. Furthermore, we identify several limitations including maintaining heterogeneity of distribution. These results, together with the framework and datasets made publicly available as R packages, will guide simulation methods selection and their future development.

We systematically compare the performance of 12 simulation methods across multiple sets of criteria, including accuracy of estimates for 13 data properties, the ability to retain biological signals and to achieve computation scalability, as well as their applicability. To assess data property estimation, we defined 13 criteria capturing both distributions of genes and cells as well as higher-order interactions, such as mean-variance relationship of genes. We developed a comparison process to quantify the similarity between simulated and real data using a kernel density based global two-sample comparison test statistic (KDE statistic). The discrepancy between two distributions is calculated based on the difference between the probability density functions, estimated via kernel smoothing. The integrated squared error (ISE) serves as the measure of discrepancy, calculated as $T=\int\left[f_1(x)-f_2(x)\right]^2~d x$, where $f_1$ and $f_2$ are kernel density estimates of sample 1 and sample 2. We applied a transformation rule to scale the absolute value of the test statistic to $[0,1]$: $x_{\text{transformed}}=\frac{|x|-\left|x_{\text{minimum}}\right|}{\left|x_{\text{maximum}}\right|-\left|x_{\text{minimum}}\right|}$, where a higher value indicates greater similarity.

For biological signals, we measured the proportion of differentially expressed (DE) genes obtained in the simulated data using DE detection methods designed for bulk (limma) and single-cell RNA-seq data (DEsingle), as well as four other types of gene signals: differentially variable (DV), differentially distributed (DD), differential proportion (DP) and bimodally distributed (BD) genes. To quantify performance, we used Symmetric Mean Absolute Percentage Error (SMAPE): $\text{SMAPE}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}\frac{\left|F_t-A_t\right|}{\frac{A_t+F_t}{2}}$, where $F_t$ is the proportion in simulated data and $A_t$ is the proportion in real data. For scalability, we measured runtime and memory usage with respect to cell numbers (50-8000 cells). For methods using a two-step process (estimation then simulation), time and memory of both steps were recorded. Tasks not finished within the time limit were assigned the maximum time and previous memory usage for ranking.

section: Results - Performance & Impact of Data Characteristics
text: No method clearly outperformed others across all criteria. ZINB-WaVE, SPARSim and SymSim performed better than others across most data properties. However, some top-performing methods like SPsimSeq and ZINB-WaVE revealed poor scalability, highlighting a trade-off between computational efficiency and modelling complexity. We examined the impact of data characteristics: regression analysis on Tabula Muris data subsets showed that increasing cell numbers correlated with more accurate estimation of properties like mean-variance relationships (positive coefficient), but negatively correlated with others like library size. For sequencing protocols (human PBMC, mouse cortex), ANOVA showed no substantial impact on simulation results, suggesting a general framework suffices for deep vs. shallow sequencing.

Comparing across criteria revealed common areas of weakness. Higher-order interactions, such as the mean-variance relationship of genes, showed larger differences between simulated and real data compared to gene- and cell-wise properties like fraction of zero per cell. Biological signals like DD and DP genes also exhibited greater differences. Many methods simulated close to zero proportions of these signals regardless of the true proportion in real data. We found none of the methods could maintain heterogeneity due to patient variability, as some methods implicitly require homogeneous input populations. The study concludes with recommendations for method selection based on the comprehensive assessment across different aspects (data property, biological signals, scalability, applicability), advising users to select methods offering functionality best suited to their purpose, such as SPARSim for large-scale simulations due to its balance of performance and scalability.
